title: Solving equations algebraically
source: Essential Mathematics for the Australian Curriculum, Year 7 (Cambridge, Greenwood et al. 2024) — Chapter 9 Equations, Section 9D. Images are direct crops of the original textbook pages.

questions.topic: identifying the operation applied to both sides of an equation

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: applying the inverse operation to only one side of the equation
misconceptions.reason: an equation stays true (equivalent) only if the exact same operation is applied to both sides - changing just one side breaks the balance

misconceptions.reason: to isolate the pronumeral you must undo the LAST operation applied first - undoing them in the original order leaves the equation more complicated, not less

questions.topic: completing the steps to solve an equation algebraically
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): 7a + 3 = 38 → (−3 both sides) → 7a = 35 → (÷7 both sides) → a = 5

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: applying the inverse operation to only one side of the equation
misconceptions.reason: an equation stays true (equivalent) only if the exact same operation is applied to both sides - changing just one side breaks the balance

questions.topic: completing the steps to solve an equation algebraically
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): 4b − 10 = 14 → (+10 both sides) → 4b = 24 → (÷4 both sides) → b = 6

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: applying the inverse operation to only one side of the equation
misconceptions.reason: an equation stays true (equivalent) only if the exact same operation is applied to both sides - changing just one side breaks the balance

questions.topic: completing the steps to solve an equation algebraically
questions.answer: 2(q + 6) = 20 → (÷2 both sides) → q + 6 = 10 → (−6 both sides) → q = 4
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): 2(q + 6) = 20 → (÷2 both sides) → q + 6 = 10 → (−6 both sides) → q = 4

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: applying the inverse operation to only one side of the equation
misconceptions.reason: an equation stays true (equivalent) only if the exact same operation is applied to both sides - changing just one side breaks the balance

questions.topic: completing the steps to solve an equation algebraically
questions.answer: 5 = x/10 + 3 → (−3 both sides) → 2 = x/10 → (×10 both sides) → 20 = x
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): 5 = x/10 + 3 → (−3 both sides) → 2 = x/10 → (×10 both sides) → 20 = x

questions.topic: solving a two-step equation algebraically and checking by substitution
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): f = 11

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: stopping at the solution without substituting it back in to check LHS = RHS
misconceptions.reason: checking by substitution confirms the solution is correct and catches arithmetic slips before they're marked wrong

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: a sign error when adding/subtracting during the undo steps, e.g. subtracting instead of adding an already-negative term
misconceptions.reason: careless handling of negative numbers during multi-step solving is the most common source of an incorrect solution

questions.topic: filling in the missing steps to show two equations are equivalent
questions.answer: 2x + 5 = 13 → (−5 both sides) → 2x = 8 → (÷2 both sides) → x = 4 → (×5 both sides) → 5x = 20
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): 2x + 5 = 13 → (−5 both sides) → 2x = 8 → (÷2 both sides) → x = 4 → (×5 both sides) → 5x = 20

questions.topic: showing two equations are equivalent by finding the missing steps
questions.answer: 10 + 2x = 20 → (−10 both sides) → 2x = 10 → (÷2 both sides) → x = 5 → (−3 both sides) → x − 3 = 2 → (×2 both sides) → 2(x − 3) = 4
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): 10 + 2x = 20 → (−10 both sides) → 2x = 10 → (÷2 both sides) → x = 5 → (−3 both sides) → x − 3 = 2 → (×2 both sides) → 2(x − 3) = 4

questions.topic: deciding if the same solution guarantees two equations are equivalent
questions.answer: Yes — if two equations each have a single solution and that solution is the same for both, the equations are equivalent.
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): Yes — if two equations each have a single solution and that solution is the same for both, the equations are equivalent.

questions.topic: deciding if different solutions guarantee two equations are not equivalent
questions.answer: Yes — if two equations have different solutions they cannot be equivalent, since equivalent equations must have exactly the same solution(s).
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): Yes — if two equations have different solutions they cannot be equivalent, since equivalent equations must have exactly the same solution(s).
